All right, you're back and it's time to plow forward now with Bandler's second leap of logic that led directly to the creation of NLP angering. Now to refresh your recollection, the first leap was the proactive nature of NLP's take on state management, namely at the recombination of your focus and by changing your physiology, you could trigger a certain state. Now the second leap of logic was to combine this with classic Pavlovian conditioning as in Pavlov's dogs. Let me ask you a question. Do you understand Pavlovian conditioning? Or would you like me to quickly explain to you? Either way is fine. Okay, great. Let's continue then. No problem. So as the story goes, something around the turn of the last century, a little-known Russian scientist named Ivan Pavlov conducted an experiment involving a starving dog, a juicy piece of meat, and an extremely loud bell. Now the protocol of the experiment was simple. Present this starving dog with a juicy piece of steak while simultaneously ringing a loud bell. And not surprisingly, the dog instantly began to salivate at the sight of the meat while the sounds of the bell was merely coincidental, at least at first. You see, what Pavlov noticed was that as he kept repeating this process over and over again, it didn't take long until the dog would just start to salivate from the sounds of the bell. The sight of the meat was no longer necessary. Now what Pavlov reasoned here was that each time you repeat the experiment, the dog's brain would develop a stronger and stronger link between the sounds of the bell and the sight of the meat until finally the link had grown so strong that the mere act of ringing the bell was powerful enough to trigger salivation. Very simple, right? Well in NLP, the bell is referred to as an anchor. The act of ringing the bell is referred to as firing off an anchor and the process which the two are linked together is referred to as setting an anchor. Now in sales, the most common state that people try to set an anchor for is the state of absolute certainty and the most common anchor they choose is to link it to a combination of shouting the word yes and simultaneously clapping their hands. Now you may have actually seen this before if you've attended any personal development seminars with someone like Tony Robbins who actually learned NLP from Dr. Baylor back in the early days. And by the way, Tony Robbins is really good at this process. In fact, if you've ever been to one of his seminars and the music is playing and the lights are flashing and the dancers are on stage dancing and the people who are only just screaming the word yes to the top of their lungs, I mean he gets people really pumped up in the moment and in that regard he's probably the best out there doing this including me. So I give him a lot of credit for that. However, that being said, if you're thinking that screaming the word yes in a crowded room while you clap your hands once isn't quite as intense as tempting a starving to death dog with a juicy piece of filet mignon while you're ringing a bell it's basically loud enough to wake the dead. Well, you're in the exact same boat I was in finding NLP anchoring only marginally effective at best and hence not really useful in the real business world. See, in point of fact, there were actually two challenges of NLP anchoring that make it very difficult for people to use it in the real world. First, is that in order to successfully set an anchor you have to be the absolute tippy top of the emotional state that you're looking to anchor in so far as the level of intensity that you feel inside at the moment you try to set it. Anything less than that and the anchor simply won't set. Now in terms of how it relates to the certainty scale from module one you have to be at an absolute unequivocal 10 with no ifs, ands, or buts about it. Only then at that precise moment the certainty is really bubbling up inside you like a seeding volcano only then can you introduce an anchor and truly set it. Conversely, if you were at an eight or even a nine which is what most people legitimately get at one of those live events where you feel great but you're not really at an absolute tippy top state of certainty, it simply won't work. So that's the first aspect of NLP anchoring that tends to trip people up. Namely, the difficulty of trying to get yourself artificially to a state of absolute certainty over that amount of absolute anything at the moment you try to set an anchor as opposed to being in that state organically. Get it? Now the second thing people struggle with with NLP anchoring is what type of anchor to choose. Well it will be the closest equivalent of the bell that can wake the dead in Pavlov's experiment that you can actually use out in the real world without freaking everybody out around you. I mean think about this, the moment before you knock on your prospect's door, you press a button in your pocket and an earth shattering siren goes off, probably not going to go very well with your prospect or anyone within 100 yards you. You get it? But still, here's the thing with an anchor. It has to not only hit you all at once, but it has to stand out in a dramatic way. A common everyday sound or gesture, it simply won't cut it. It needs to be an extreme one and the more extreme the better and also the more unusual the better. In essence, you want it to use something that will actually hit you up right in an unforgettable way and literally shock your senses. That's what a great anchor does and it's absolutely crucial that you have one. But still, despite these challenges with NLP anchoring, are you at the science behind NLP's approach with sound and at the payoff for cracking the code would be massive? Not so much for me, but for the countless people who attend my seminars and live events around the world. You see, here's the deal. I personally have the ability to manage my state naturally at a very high level, but I'm an oddball. For every person like me, there's 10 million more people who are the exact opposite and they struggle with managing their emotional state. Well, here's the deal. As I said, I finally hit on a way to do it and I called it olfactory anchoring and it's exactly what I'm going to be teaching you in the next lesson. So let's have a short break here. Do your quiz, go through your exercises and when you're done, meet me in the next lesson. I'll see you there.
